1: A type: “Hear this word, you cows of Bashan on Mount Samaria — you who oppress the poor and crush the needy and say to your husbands, ‘Bring us something to drink!’” Bashan was famous for its fattened livestock (Psalms 22:12: “strong bulls of Bashan surround me”). Amos applies the image to Samaria’s wealthy women who drive their husbands to oppress the poor in order to fund their feasting. The NT counterpart: the parable of the rich fool who says to his soul, “Eat, drink, be merry” (Luke 12:19), oblivious that the poor are dying at his gate. Christ’s beatitude — “blessed are the poor” — is the reversal of the Bashan-cow prosperity ethic.
2: A shadow: “The Lord GOD has sworn by his holiness: days are coming when they will drag you away with hooks — even the last of you with fishhooks.” The oath by God’s holiness is the strongest form of divine guarantee — YHWH’s own character is pledged to the judgment. The fishermen-imagery (hooks/fishhooks) is reversed in Christ’s call to his disciples: “Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men” (Matthew 4:19). Amos’s hooks catch the wealthy for judgment; Christ’s nets catch the humble for salvation — the same imagery in precisely inverted operation.
3: A shadow: the women of Samaria will be led out through breaches in the wall and thrown onto a dunghill. The imagery of being expelled through broken walls — the very walls built to protect privilege — is the eschatological pattern of judgment: what you built to protect your wealth becomes the route of your exile. Christ’s parable of the banquet (Luke 14:21-24) has a similar logic: those invited from positions of privilege exclude themselves; the poor and crippled and lame enter through the open doors.
4: A type: “Come to Bethel and rebel; go to Gilgal and multiply your rebellion.” The sarcastic invitation — come to the sanctuaries and sin — is prophetic irony at its sharpest. Both Bethel and Gilgal were sites of Israel’s earliest covenant history that had been corrupted into centers of false worship. Jesus uses the same ironic mode with the Pharisees: “You travel across sea and land to make a single proselyte, and when he becomes a proselyte, you make him twice as much a child of hell as yourselves” (Matthew 23:15). Religious observance without covenant faithfulness multiplies guilt rather than reducing it.
5: A type: “Offer your leavened thanksgiving sacrifices... announce your freewill offerings publicly.” The public display of religiosity is what YHWH mocks: “for so you love to do, O people of Israel!” The love of visible piety is precisely what Jesus condemns: “Beware of practicing your righteousness before other people in order to be seen by them” (Matthew 6:1). Leavened bread was explicitly excluded from the temple offerings (Leviticus 7:13 allows it for certain thanksgiving offerings but the point here is the corruption of the whole system). Religious performance without relational covenant is the target of both Amos and Jesus.
6: A type: “I gave you empty mouths in all your cities and shortage of food in all your towns — yet you did not return to me, declares the LORD.” The refrain “yet you did not return to me” (wĕlōʾ-shavtem ʿaday) repeats through Amos 4:6-11 — five cycles of warning-without-repentance. The NT applies this pattern to Jesus’s own generation: “He performed signs among them, yet they still did not believe in him” (John 12:37). The hardening of those who refuse to return despite judgment is the pattern that Paul analyzes in Romans 9-11 using Pharaoh as the paradigm (Romans 9:17-18).
7: A shadow: “I also withheld the rain from you three months before harvest; I sent rain on one city but not another.” The selective withdrawal of rain — one city rain, another dry — is YHWH’s providential discipline. The NT uses weather-imagery for both blessing and judgment: “He makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust” (Matthew 5:45 — general blessing); but also “I have this against you: you have abandoned the love you had at first... I will come to you and remove your lampstand from its place” (Revelation 2:4-5 — selective removal of blessing). Christ’s parable of the Prodigal’s elder brother lives in plenty while the younger son famishes — the same within-family economic differentiation as discipline.
8: A shadow: “Two or three cities staggered to another city for water and could not get enough — yet you did not return to me.” The desperate seeking of water that cannot satisfy is the image Christ uses in the opposite direction: “whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again” (John 4:14). Israel staggers to cisterns that are dry (Jeremiah 2:13 uses the same image); Jesus offers the spring of living water. The inability to return to YHWH under discipline is resolved only by Christ, who makes return possible.
9: A shadow: blight, mildew, and locusts consuming gardens and vineyards — covenant curses from Deuteronomy 28:22, 38-42 arriving exactly as warned. The vine-and-garden imagery runs throughout the OT and NT as a symbol of Israel’s covenant life. Jesus says, “I am the true vine” (John 15:1) — the antidote to the blighted vine of Amos; branches that abide in him bear fruit rather than being devoured by locusts. “Yet you did not return to me” — the refrain signals that only Christ, not discipline alone, produces genuine return.
10: A shadow: “I sent among you a plague like those of Egypt; I killed your young men with the sword, took your horses... yet you did not return to me.” The Egypt-plague comparison places Israel under the same judgment that fell on Pharaoh — the ultimate irony, since YHWH brought Israel out of Egypt and its plagues (Amos 2:10). The NT sees Christ as the one who passes through death-as-plague and emerges alive — the true Passover Lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7) who absorbs the plague-judgment that Israel continues to face under Amos.
11: A type: “I overthrew some of you as God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah; you were like a stick snatched from the fire.” The Sodom-reference is the most extreme judgment-comparison available in the OT. Christ uses the same comparison: “I tell you, it will be more bearable on that day for Sodom than for that town” (Luke 10:12 — for towns that reject the apostles). The “stick snatched from the fire” becomes in Zechariah 3:2 the image of Joshua the high priest rescued from Satan — and ultimately of the elect who are snatched from the fire of judgment (Jude 23: “save others by snatching them out of the fire”).
12: A fulfillment-anticipation: “Therefore, because I will do this to you, O Israel, prepare yourself to meet your God.” The eschatological summons — prepare to meet God — is both threat and, for the repentant, invitation. The NT takes this up as the call to readiness for Christ’s return: “Therefore, stay awake, for you do not know on what day your Lord is coming” (Matthew 24:42). Meeting God in Amos is a prospect of judgment; meeting Christ in the NT is either terror or joy depending on one’s standing before him. The preparation Christ calls for is faith and faithfulness, not merely fearful trembling.
13: A fulfillment: “For this is he who forms the mountains and creates the wind, who reveals to humanity what he is thinking, who turns dawn into darkness and treads on the heights of the earth — the LORD God of Armies is his name.” This hymnic doxology attributes creation, revelation, and cosmic sovereignty to YHWH. The NT applies all three attributes to Christ: creator (John 1:3: “all things were made through him”; Colossians 1:16); revealer (John 1:18; Hebrews 1:2: “through whom also he created the world” and “in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son”); and sovereign over creation (Matthew 8:27: “even the winds and sea obey him”). Amos’s creator-God and the NT’s Christ are identified.
